sourceDesc.p: Originally posted as a series of posts on Medium.com, this text has been translated from HTML to TEI, and combined to form a single document.

body.p: At the end of my sixth decade, having some credit at my bank, and no particular calls on my time, I resolved to spend a week or two travelling by slow train from left to right along the shores of the Bay of Biscay, in the company of my oldest surviving friend Dr G.A.

We began the journey proper in Santiago de Compostela and finished it in Hendaye, so this may be thought of as a kind of reverse or widdershins pilgrimage. To get to Santiago, we took a high speed train from Madrid; and from Hendaye we took a high speed train to Paris. But in between we were on FEVE trains, which is a very different kind of experience. (See http://www.wikiwand.com/en/Renfe_Feve for background on FEVE)
We took it in turns to write a daily log, which was first published on Medium, and is now for the first time collected as a single document.

ref.hi: FEVE fever : 1
In which we find the Holy Grail
FEVE Fever : 2
In which we pass by the end of the world
FEVE fever : 3
The vendetta of the invincible Armada
FEVE fever : 4
In which we return to the big(gish) City
FEVE fever : 5
In my beginning is my end
FEVE fever : 6
In which we are translated from Asturian to Castilian
FEVE fever : 7
A million Mexicans can’t be wrong: the Transcantabrian Adventure
FEVE fever : 8
In which we reach the (other) end of the line
FEVE fever : 9
Goodbye Feve; Hail and Farewell Euskotren.
FEVE fever : 10
In which we glide back into the 21st century

head.hi: In which we find the Holy Grail

div.p: Guy sez: What can one say about the 9.15 8-coach ALVIA Talgo from platform 21 of Madrid Chamartin for Ferrol, calling at Zamora, Ourense, Santiago de Compostella, A Coruna, and Betanzos?

figure.head: The rain in Spain falls mainly on the train

div.p: Very little really, and I doubt much more tomorrow when we pick it up again from Santiago to Ferrol. Miles of empty countryside which give way to single-track snow-lined passes and peaks after the ritual of gauge change. A friendly guard who shows us where we are on the map. An anxious glance at the speedometer as we come to the curve leading into Santiago where a train derailed last year. Virtually no other traffic.

Lou was offered an Eeyorish dinner at Pepe’s on arrival last night, branded (in the English menu) as beans and thistles. But with no sign of the thistles in practice. I finished my nice acorn-fed ham boccadillo in my stuffy little room. We depart from Chamartin not too much the worse for wear with Burnard family blessings.

Lunch in the first restaurant we encounter coming out of the station at Santiago. Galician soup proves a splendid invention. And then we set about hunting for the Holy Grail, located after some brief confusion under a dusky portico. A charming elderly establishment, with old wooden floors, much wooden pannelling, low ceilings, and walls too thin to keep out the rhythm section rehearsing next door.
Destination the cathedral. We come to a hat shop. Lou needs a new beret — cheaper here than in France, it seems. Both emerge wearing berets.

figure.head: Guy is fitted for his beret (slightly smaller than mine hem hem)

div.p: We proceed to purchase obligatory tourist tat, e.g. postcards, snow globes, etc. etc. Electric candles in front of St James’ relics on behalf of Sandra and Adolfo. But I draw the line at joining the queue to embrace the saint’s statue from the rear, so can tell you nothing today about his bottom. His main claim to fame (apart from apostolic status and biblical references) appears to have been helping Alfonso the nth to defeat the Moors in 700 and something. Will try and find out more about this guest appearance in history. Certainly convenient that his martyred remains had been imported from the Middle East for the occasion. Expecting to find floods of pilgrims, but there seem relatively few tourists around, and between showers we wander round bookshops (- Do you have a book called Transcantabrica. I don’t know the author or publisher. — No.: this allusion reserved exclusively for the surviving Pixies among our readers.)

Dinner is in what we gather is a former gentlemen’s club across the road, with large armchairs and carved wood panelling mainly but not exclusively depicting naked ladies (the panelling that is). If my guts would only behave I might appreciate it more. As it is, I give up on my second Galician soup of the day and on Lou’s surfeit of small green peppers, and retire for an early night: Lou shortly knocks clutching consolatory bottles of water and clementines. More tomorrow, hopefully not of the same, from Lou.

div.head: In which we pass by the end of the world
div.p: Lou says: One (or two) good night’s sleep later, we return to the Cafe Casino, partly to see whether it is just as extraordinary by daylight, but mostly to enjoy its excellent coffee and selections of desayunos (No 2 for G, No 3 for L). These comprise crunchy grainy toast smeared with salsa and (in the case of No 3) large quantities of ham. And did I mention the excellent restorative coffee? A flock of tourists comes in out of the rain, and is sent packing by the waiter, hoorah.

figure.head: Death and the Maiden at the Cafe Casino

div.p: We have time to purchase more schmuck and effectuate a further modicum of tourism, so we take in the Cathedral museum, which turns out to be largely an advertisement for — or testimony to — the heroic efforts of the restoration workers, but it does feature some impressive pieces of devotional kitsch, such as you might expect to have accumulated after a few centuries

figure.head: Hi Mary! Who’s your friend?

div.p: And so back the Hotel of the Holy Graal to finish packing and watch the world go by for a few moments from my window, before paying up and moving on. I would be happy to return here, for one. It’s noon and far too early to expect anyone to serve lunch, but we do eventually persuade the cerviceria opposite the Galician parliament to oblige. The dish of the day is bacalao, which seems too good to resist. Guy’s innards are still feeling tender, but he is persuaded by the solicitous barman to order only half a steak. And so back to the station in good time for the 1417 train to Ferrol, the same one as we got off of yesterday, or not, depending on your views about the nature of identity.

The train chunters through the glooming (is that a word?) to A Coruna while I wrestle with the problem of reorganizing the photos I have taken on the trip so far, and do not therefore notice that the train has had to reverse direction to back out of A Coruna, before continuing to chunter more slowly into the rather grim estuary of Betanzos-Infesta, and then back out again in the opposite direction. The rain continues to fall as it skirts the edge of the estuary until it finds a bridge to cross over and descend on the charming city of Ferrol, our destination du jour. From which, Guy informs me, the Spanish armada sailed some time ago.

Ferrol station is where our real FEVE adventure is due to begin tomorrow, since this is the westernmost terminus of the narrow-gauge lines we are taking along the coast towards Bilbao. A helpful lady at the station explains to us the rules and regulations for buying FEVE tickets (only on the day of departure, please, and only for one section at a time), and also reveals the cost benefits of buying a Golden Card, the Spanish equivalent of the Senior Railcard or Carte Senior. 6 euros for a year’s half price travel is an irresistible bargain in any language.

And the sun comes out! Our hotel today is a charming edifice dating from 1906 on one of the three pedestrianized shopping streets that constitute much of the centre of Ferrol, originally laid out on a grid pattern (Guy tells me) during the Enlightenment to house shipyard workers. We inspect the naval base, or more exactly the wall around it and the fish market (closed), and also some tumbling down houses on the cobbled precipitous streets of the Old Town before resting in a park full of peacocks and seagulls. The evocative cries of the seagulls are interspersed with the low comedy of the peacocks. And thence for a light supper (mmm sardines, and patatas bravas) in a typical Galician meson.

figure.head: For low comedy, it is hard to better the peacock’s arse

div.head: The vendetta of the invincible Armada
div.p: El Ferrol, where I was awakened at dawn this morning by the wheeling of gulls outside my French window (following three prior awakenings by other calls of nature), was razed virtually to the ground by fire in 1568. Nonetheless some accounts claim that, as the home of the Spanish Arsenal, it was still a point of departure for the invincible fleet in 1588.

figure.head: Invincible Armada approaching the Isle of White (from our own reporter) via Wikimedia

div.p: Drake’s observations have gone down in history as relating to a game of bowls, but Elizabethan spelling was clearly flexible, certainly on Plymouth Hoe, so it is quite likely that the official record missed the vowel e. Some of the Spanish ships will have visited Mexico earlier in the century, and have encountered and duly imported to the Arsenal significant samples of Montezuma’s revenge. Vengeance proved total last night in the (quite nice) Galician bar where Lou succeeded, but I totally failed to hold down (or up) my dinner, something I do not intend to try to — again until I have departed this place. As I now write the following evening, when we have finally left Galicia and entered Asturias, perhaps limited risks may be taken. It will be seen.

After this brief historical-autobiographical preface, we turn to the details of walking to the railway station(s) in Ferrol, via two bookshops neither of which has a book called Transcantabrica (even though we now know the author and publisher). Half the station being RENFE, half being FEVE (Ferrocarriles Españoles de Vía Estrecha = Spanish narrow gauge railways), which is now also owned by RENFE (This means that the national Spanish rail operator somehow manages to run trains on three different gauges, specifically the 1668-mm “Iberian gauge”, the 1435-mm “European gauge” and the 1000-mm “Metre Gauge” networks). Access to the two sets of platforms is strictly segregated: to get to a FEVE platform you must have a FEVE ticket. This also provides access to the FEVE half of the station bar, occupied by a number of railwaymen, where we order European toasted sandwiches to accompany the inevitable freshly squeezed orange juice and coffee, and then watch the 1030 departure move out from platform 7 prior to embarking on our 1045 from platform 5.

figure.head: 1045 Ferrol-Ribadeo preparing to leave

div.p: There is an intensive service on this route for the first few miles, which progressively thins out to become a mere two trains a day beyond Ribadeo, our current destination which we reach some three hours later. All the trains are two-coach articulated diesel multiple units with driver and guard which stop upon request: so we speed happily through the first station from Ferrol before braking dramatically and reversing into the station to pick up a previously unnoticed would-be passenger.

The route is decidedly scenic, with lots of little farms, and many wooded hills covered in eucalyptus (on which more anon). There are also lots of crossing points with station masters/mistresses in smart hats (they aren’t the only ones: see first instalment). The train averages 4–5 passengers until we get to the last stretch along the coast to Ribadeo, where we are joined by a vociferous group who seem the worse/better for drink. At Ribadeo everyone gets out, including the train crew who are taken away in a car, presumably to lunch.

figure.head: Buenos dias Ribadeo. Bring us lunch.

div.p: We share this objective, and a five-minute walk takes us to O Piano, and another Galician soup of a fishy nature, though I can’t handle the boiled beef which follows it on the Menu del Dia.

Somewhat restored, we set out for the town centre, where we find: a post office without anywhere to post things; another Casino in need of repair, the glimmering red and silver tiles of Ribadeo’s triumph of Art Nouveau.

figure.head: Casino in urgent need of restoration, Ribadeo

div.p: I have completely run out of energy at this point, but Lou manages to get me back to the railway station and purchase tickets for Navia, via a supermarket and a supply of bananas and fruit juice. Ribadeo is the last place in Galicia, and perhaps of associated maledictions. Into the train, seated opposite a fluffy Spanish lady whom the guard assumes is English, and here we all are in Navia (accent on first syllable), walking down the hill to our (fairly) luxurious hotel wherein I have the dingy desk with hi-tech bathroom facilities, and Lou has the luxurious suite complete with view over the estuary and tailors dummy.

Dinner, for which the hotel cannot be held responsible, is a total disaster. I am a wreck after struggling through the streets of Ribadeo with zero energy. For Lou it is far too late for eating. We end up stuck between two screens displaying the evening’s football game at full volume in an unpleasant and largely deserted bar. My two glasses of wine do however remain in situ, and we stagger back to the hotel and fall over. If only they would invest in something over a five-watt light bulb. Good nite.

div.head: In which we return to the big(gish) City
div.p: Lou says: I am woken by the sound of rain falling on the slate roof of my penthouse suite at the Casona Naviega, and also by occasional swooshes of motor transport along the road outside. How nice to be staying in bed this morning. Yes, the sky is very grey, with no evident signs of improvement, though the view across the estuary is still quite pleasant.

figure.head: View from the Casona Naviega, top floor

div.p: Excellent breakfast awaits downstairs, so I heave myself out by 10 or so, and graze appreciatively for a while before prodding G into making an appearance. The hotelier converses in broken English, first with another guest, and then with us, at some length. He knows Oxford colleges well, but from the perspective of the migrant Spanish waiter. We leave him hastily to catch our 1225 train, which is in the event 10 minutes late arriving.

It is a shame about the endless rain, since the scenery we are pottering through is very scenic. We are travelling parallel to the coast line, and therefore of necessity having to cope with numerous inlets, estuaries, and heaps of rock in inconvenient places. Which means that there are lots of deep cuttings, dizzy embankments, and wiggly tunnels for the train to navigate. Every now and then we also see snake-like motorways swooping across gorges we are compelled to chug gingerly around or under, lifted up high on enormous concrete pillars.

In between, we occasionally catch a brief perspective down a valley to a stormy beach, but mostly masses of gumtrees. My researches indicate that the eucalyptus is now the preferred crop in these parts, which means much debate about its ecological impact, need for careful management to avoid fires, and general political hoo hah. It’s a profitable crop that doesn’t require industrial scale farming, is my understanding. To celebrate which, here is some lumberjack porn showing exactly how to turn a giant eucalyptus into a massive beam.
The train, by the way, is another two-car diesel unit, but a bit more modern and comfortable, so that everyone on board soon dozes off.

figure.head: 1330 arrival at Pravia

div.p: We arrive at Pravia (not to be confused with Navia) without incident, and detrain in search of lunch. The station master assuring us that this is only available in town, we leave the station via a lift, and five minutes later are walking up Pravia’s main street which does indeed boast several eating establishments as well as some colourful architecture. Lunch is fish soup for Guy, and salad followed by yet more ham and potatoes. But the waiter is friendly and the ambiance agreeable. Small town vibes suit us old timers well.

And then back down the hill we go to pick up our onward train to Gijon. This is an electrified item, since we are now in the metropolitan area of Gijon, and the view from the window is remarkably different. Around the port of Aviles and beyond we pass through an industrial wasteland, featuring huge gas and coke processors, some of them rusting and defunct, others just rusting. There is a brief interlude of green dotted with what appear to be small seaside villas, but then the industrial zone reasserts itself, as for example the Zone of Distribution, where lorries and containers are massed for despatch throughout Europe, some of them wrapped up in plastic like the luggage of nervous air travellers. As we trundle into Gijon there is mile upon mile of the sort of featureless industrial estates you might see at the periphery of almost any large European or American city. For Gijon, as we realise when we arrive, is actually a city, with long calles full of shops, some of them pedestrianized, there are plazas beneath which there are car parks, so as to permit the pedestrianization, and there’s a pleasing jumble of ancient and modern architecture. It’s a long schlep into town and to our hotel, but instructive.

figure.head: Backstreet Downtown Gijon

div.p: After a brief nap, we take a pleasant walk along the promenade before follow the advice of the hotel map in selecting from the dozens of cidrerias along the streets around the harbour. This one aereates the cider not by means of theatrical gestures but by means of an ingenious kind of stopper to the bottle. The waiter also persuades us to try some freshly grilled fish with it, which turns out to be a very good idea. And so, dear reader, to bed.

div.head: In my beginning is my end
div.p: Guy sez: Yes, today is the only day to finish where it starts. It thus involves a return journey. After finding breakfast near the bus station, we proceed beneath the rain to various chemists lacking appropriate syringes and, eventually, the Railway Museum in Gijon, which now occupies the original railway station building . The latter celebrates, inter alia, the birth of the Gijon-Langreo railway in 1852.

figure.head: Opening of the Gijon-Langreo Railway, courtesy An Unknown Artist and Wikimedia

div.p: Unlike any other railway in Spain for the next century, this was built to standard gauge (1.438m), before being forcibly converted to 1m gauge in the 1960s to become part of FEVE — a little before the Spanish AVE project was formulated to introduce standard gauge as the high speed norm.

The museum hosts many fine relics of narrow gauge splendour including a yellow FEVE travelling post office, various tank engines and 4-wheel coaches, which we gaze at in the rain for a while. It also has a splendid and extensive model railway of 00 gauge staffed by middle-aged spaniards happily whiling away their Saturday morning.

figure.head: If the train risks running into the building, just move the building…

div.p: Aside from the proper gauge for railways, the main existential problem around these parts appears to be how to pour cider. This has to be aerated prior to consumption. The traditional means is by pouring into a glass held in one hand by the knee from a bottle held in the other hand as far above the head as possible. The possibilities of inaccuracy are considerable, hence best done either outside or over a large bucket. Only a small amount has to be poured at a time — certainly not more than 100ml — so that it does not fall flat before consumption. At dinner yesterday we were introduced to the first technological solution, namely a bottle top which magically mixes air with the cider as it is poured. Today, we encountered the electronic version, where at the end of each table is positioned a device in which you place your glass at an angle of 30 degrees in a holder, brandish your swipe card, and wait for replenishment, as an illuminated display informs you how much you have consumed (or not) from the hidden supply pipe.

This is in Oviedo, which we have reached on an express FEVE 2-coach electric multiple unit from Gijon, taking a mere 30 minutes through a lengthy double-track tunnel (previously home to an inclined plane operating on the principle that the trains of coal descending were heavier than those of empties ascending), and whose city centre is full of cranes and closed roads before we finally reach our reference point, namely a statue of Woody Allen, who must have a weakness for cider.

figure.head: Watch out Woodie, she’s checking up on you with Google

div.p: Lunch takes place on Cider Boulevard in Oviedo, and consists of fabada (large white beans stewed with bits of sausage) as well as our half-dozen cider top/bottom-ups. The cathedral is shut. We wander round the closed market and sit in the park as it starts raining again. No, more serious matters still await, in the shape of Lou’s trews. These had been looming from various shop windows in Gijon, and after a restorative cuppa, here we are at a mere few yards from El Corte Ingles (Oviedo branch), where a girth-attentive lady on the second floor resolves the problem by trial and error for a mere thirty euros, thereby enabling us to subsequently rush through the rain and catch the 4.40 back to Gijon.

Here Lou will subsequently seek out and test the laundry facilities (another advantage of remaining in the same place for two nights in a row), and I will fall asleep for three hours feeling knackered after discovering that the hotel laundry service hasn’t. Note: this last part of the journey also involved our first Spanish taxi, hardly a matter of note. But there we were, and it was pissing it down as it had been all day.
And the evening and that evening’s cider, which we watched being poured by a charming lady while we drank wine, were the fifth day.

div.head: In which we are translated from Asturian to Castilian
div.p: Lou says: A pleasant breakfast in the Hernan Cortes hotel before we shoulder our bags and decamp for Gijon’s nasty new station once more. At least it’s not actually raining, though it soon will be. Today we are going to leave the Asturias definitively and head deep inland to Leon in search of the legendary once-a-day Leon-Bilbao FEVE line. We have tickets on the RENFE service to Alacant, on which one carriage is considered to be a “regionale” (i.e. cheap) service for poor folks like us, but only on Sundays. Which it is, plus we have our Golden Cards as aforesaid, so we get to ride at the front of the train for very cheap, hoorah. We head for approximately the same valley as we did on yesterday’s trip to Oviedo, but along entirely different tracks, thus affording a view of the opposite side of the gas-coke-processing complex outside Oviedo. Our train continues beyond there towards the coal mining town of La Robla, which involves going over some serious mountain ranges and (today at least) into the clouds. For yes, it is now raining again and visibility is pretty much the same whether we are inside or outside the numerous twisty little tunnels the train clatters through as it goes up the valley out of Asturias and then down on the other side into Castille y Leon. Guy remains convinced that we will at some point enter a new more direct tunnel, scheduled to replace the numerous twisty little tunnels, but it is clearly still scheduled for manana. Before starting the descent and just above the cloudline the scenery mutates into jagged rock formations, with snow lying on the ground, and very little by way of vegetation or villages. But soon, round about Gordo country, we are speeding through a valley that looks (by contrast) more like a lush flood plain with big meadows full of contented cows once more. And the sun is coming out again!

Leon RENFE station turns out to be another nasty new one, about fifty metres away from the perfectly good but now defunct old one. I have been here before (last summer with Lilette and a huge suitcase) so I know the way into town, though not much more since all we did on that occasion was have lunch. We cross the river and walk about 15 minutes into the old town, where we are greeted by a large roman pillar topped by a stork’s nest complete with storks.

There are (according to Guy’s Guidebook) three essential architectural treasures in this city: the Cathedral, the College of St Isidore, and St Mark’s church, representing the Gothic, the Classical, and the Renaissance periods respectively. Our hotel is conveniently located in the Roman part i.e. inside the former monastery attached to the Church of St Isidore, patron saint of the Internet as any fule kno. It boasts the usual sort of stone corridors and cloisters, monastic cells complete with crosses on the wall, numerous art works, nice comfy chairs at regular intervals, and really bad wifi. But our cells look reasonably comfortable and there is real linen on the beds.

However, we urgently need lunch and sally forth in search of same. It is Sunday and the town is full of similarly motivated families on their way back from mass, so it takes a while to find somewhere with space to accomodate us. The beef goulasch and potatoes were definitely worth the wait though.

figure.head: A decent meal at last

div.p: Replete, we take a brief digestive stroll in order to locate the FEVE station (thank you Google maps) which is shut, and the market, ditto, before retiring to our cells for a well earned siesta.

When I wake, I spend an hour or so getting annoyed by the inadequacies of the hotel wifi (which dear reader is why yesterday’s blog appeared 24 hours late) and we then set off on a quixotic search for something really light for supper. And a drink, There is no problem finding drink: the bars are all doing very nicely thank you, and the clientele is agreeably mixed in age, but the one we settle on offers as free munchies only crisps, olives, or something called garlic soup. So we order raciones again (Guy has a plate of cheesy fried croquettes, and I have a mass of fried sweetbreads) which is more than we can eat, even with the accompaniment of a rather nice fresh white wine. But on the way back to the cloister, we find a tiny bar which proffers grilled mushrooms to accompany the glass of brandy I had proposed as night cap. And we begin to feel that maybe we can cope with spanish life after all. And so to bed.

div.head: A million Mexicans can’t be wrong: the Transcantabrian Adventure
div.p: Guy says : We inspected the FEVE station in Leon (closed on Sundays) last night, where a substitute bus driver assured us it would be open on Monday. As indeed it was when we went down after breakfast, but no trains apparent. Substitute buses as far as the university, apparently, on account of the Obras. And thereafter, finally, there would be trains. The ticket office lady gives us tickets and timetables for the one train each day covering the Leon-Bilbao route, scheduled to take a mere eight hours for the 250 miles. We aim to get off after a mere seven hours at Balmaseda, where the Bilbao suburban service terminates. Our morning plans for Leon therefore include substantial shopping with a visit to the market to procure adequate supplies for the journey.

But first, a tour of the church of St Isidore, with its beguiling 12th century frescoes of the months and miscellaneous saints, and many tombs of the kings and queens of Leon. This leads us back to our collegio (also dedicated to St Isidore), where a number of fellow guests are awaiting the promised free conducted tour. However, having been talked at in Castilian for a few minutes and inspected the Roman walls, where the mystery of Leon’s ubiquitous cockerel is explained, we defect: we still have the cathedral to see. So we finish packing and deposit our belongings, at a point to prove key to a miraculous discovery some two hours later.
Off to the cathedral, which boasts much stained glass, some of it medieval, in Gothic windows. Free audio guides educate us as to the spiritual significance and purity of Gothic Architecture.

figure.head: Err, Mary, you seem to have a bun in the oven

div.p: And then it’s time for coffee and the market, where the one open stall convinces Lou of its wildly expensive ham and cheese while I go to the bread shop and also pick up olives, water, and wine for which the lady lends a corkscrew. St Isidore has become, Lou assures me, the patron saint of the internet. Could this be the reason why, when we collect our bags from the Collegio before retracing our tracks to the FEVE station, we find a serrated knife miraculously hidden behind Lou’s bag, ideal for cutting bread, cheese, and fruit on lengthy train journeys when devising bloggery? Finally, a digression upon the nests of storks, of which one reposes outside St Isidore’s. During the day we find these may be comfortably situated on top of a) pillars; b) derelict industrial chimneys; c) telegraph poles, as well as trees of course. To be defended against other marauding storks by rapid beak-clicking, not unlike the pecking of woodpeckers.

After an extensive bus tour of the university, we board the train for Bilbao. Half an hour later, we reach the junction for La Robla (where the coal came from), and the line turns to the east, skirting the Asturian/Cantabrian peaks on the south side. This means that while generally heading east, it turns north up any river valley encountered, which it then descends slowly to the first appropriate crossing place (generally coinciding with a village) before returning south along the other side of the valley until it has reacquired sufficient height to cross the next ridge; and then repeat the entire operation for the next valley. This means, roughly speaking, ten kilometres of labour for every two moved eastwards. There are no tunnels, no fancy viaducts, in fact nothing but long curving lines of ballast, most of it with 50km/h speed limits, presumably placed there when the line was reopened with European funding some 20 years ago.

We say hello to numerous cows, and few signs of human habitation. We spot more storks (or are they cranes), we startle wild deer, and more implausibly cattles, horses, and donkeys. The stations are nearly all request stops; the crossing points have red-hatted station mistresses/masters; we occasionally cross a descending train for Leon starting from Guardo, a mere 3 hours up the line and where everything (except our train) terminates. As well as the three staff in the driving cab, and the third different guard/ticket collector since we started, there are three other passengers as we gradually move closer to the bare snow-capped peaks, and up above the tree line. Until finally, after four hours, we find ourselves running alongside the broad gauge Burgos-Santander line into Mataporquera

figure.head: Mataporquera. Note the cement factory.

div.p: Mataporquera is the nearest thing to an event on the journey. First, it is the only point where you could in fact change and get onto another railway line. Second, it is the crossing point with the train in the other direction, for which we have to wait a few minutes. This gives us time to stretch our legs and examine the group of oldies hanging around the platform, and follow their indications to the waiting room cum railway museum, which primarily documents the lives of the train crews in the days of steam and their cooking skills. Mataporquera boasts a large cement works, and numerous wagons (of both gauges), but nothing resembling an active goods train. We agree it would not have been a thrilling place to spend the night, even if it does apparently boast two hotels.
We move on. Snow-capped peaks give way to long cliff-topped mountains and cows to sheep as we wander around a long reservoir and then drop down to Balmaseda.

figure.head: Cliff topped mountains

div.p: Balmaseda is home to the FEVE railway works, and the apparently luxurious La Robla tourist train, and is also where we are staying tonight in a former nunnery. Which, when we find it, is apparently closed for the night. The museum next door provides advice, leading us to the back door of the hotel (also closed) and then phoning them. Wait five minutes and someone will come. A somewhat harassed-looking young lady eventually turns up, checks us in, and departs. The rooms are reasonably comfortable, for a nunnery.

We wander back through the town to the main square exchanging childhood railway memories and looking for somewhere to eat, but nine o’clock is clearly too late for anything other than a glass of Rioja and a sandwich. It’s been a long day, and this is the Basque country.
PS We never found that Transcantabrica book, either.

div.head: In which we reach the (other) end of the line
div.p: Lou says: I apologise to the noble Basques, but their language defeats me totally. It seems to be mostly composed of the names of minor characters in bad science fiction novels, e.g. “Norabide Guztiak”. Still, the sun is shining and we are getting by reasonably well with pidgin spanish and much hand waving. Nothing however had quite prepared me for the assault on the senses this morning when I poked my head out of my cell in search of breakfast. There is a VERY LOUD tape of VERY BAD torch singing in International Amurrican booming out incongruously across the decorative courtyard of the Convento San Roque. I escape from it into the bar, where I consume some coffee (reasonable), some orange juice (fake), a croissant (fresh, but sugary), and a piece of freshly made tortilla (quite nice).

Guy appears and we take a pleasant walk in the Spring Sunshine, first over a rather scenic medieval bridge and up the road to inspect the nearest railway station (La Calzada, terminus of the Bilbao metro) and then back along the river to inspect the main square of Balmaseda. Apparently, this town was big in the 12 century when the populace hit on the cunning wheeze of charging itinerant merchants for the privilege of crossing the river by means of their rather scenic medieval bridge, but subsequently declined when a new road was constructed between Biscay and Navarre in the 16th century. It recovered slightly at the end of the 19th century when the La Robla railway arrived and is now poised for, well, whatever comes along really. Speaking for myself, I recommend the baker in the main square, whose chocolate and buns are both excellent. There is also a gothic church if you like that sort of thing.

figure.head: Balmaseda’s renowned medieval bridge

div.p: Our train journey today is essentially a continuation of the epic trip we did yesterday, although it is on a train which considers itself to be part of the Greater Bilbao metropolitan area. It’s just like any other commuter train, except that for most of the way we are descending a wooded river valley, with occasional pasturage and villages. As we descend, industrial sites and housing developments become more frequent, though still interspersed with spots of more bucolic charm, until finally as we arrive at the fringes of Bilbao proper they become entirely dominant.

The FEVE stations also become correspondingly grander as we approach, boasting such luxuries as multiple platforms, escalators and so forth. And within 40 minutes here we are back in the modern(ish) world at Bilbao’s beautiful Concordia station (“eclecticism, 1902”), where the lines symbolically terminate under the watchful eye of portuguese venture capitalist Victor de Chavarri to whom is attributed the immortal phrase “El ferrocarril ademas in historia: tambien es arte” in a plaque commemorating the terminus’s 90th birthday in 1992.

figure.head: O mama, can this really be the end?

div.p: And so we reach the end of the FEVE. Or is it? Because here in the Basque country, there is another narrow gauge network called the “Euskotren” which formed part of the FEVE network up till the 1980s, when the Spanish government handed it over to the provincial government in a fit of devolution. And tomorrow we will continue our journey on that line from its Atxuri terminus, on the other side of the old town.

For now, however, it’s time to find our cheap hotel (booked by Guy at the last minute last night, but none the worse for that), and then (a recurrent theme I know) some lunch. Both are adjacent in Maria Somebody street, in the heart of the pedestrianised old town. The former glories in a curvaceous wooden staircase, and the latter (recommended by the former) is in a typical back room comedor, featuring nice artichokes, thin slices of grilled meats, a scattering of chips, and a bottle of rather good wine which we cannot quite finish, before retiring to see Esther, bless her.

Around 5 pm, as per usual, we re-arise and explore further. Our plan is to stroll along the river bank to the Guggenheim museum though (I am informed) this is more impressive on the outside than the inside. The outside certainly is grandiose in an entirely post-modernistic way; see just about any architectural journal. Our energies are not equal however to the task of exploring its expensive insides, and since it is starting to rain again we take a tram back into town instead. And then we went out and ate rice, in the form of vegetable paella, and consumed a refreshing bottle of young red wine. And it was good. No, really, really good. Notwithstanding our neighbours slowly and noisily scoffing a large piece of grilled cow in the background.

div.head: Goodbye Feve; Hail and Farewell Euskotren.
div.p: Guy says: Our efficiency when we have a train to catch is astounding, in this case the 11 am from Bilbao Atxari to Donostia. For which, we discovered yesterday, senior railcards give no reduction whatever. A 9am gesture at breakfast in the hotel, who present us each with a neatly wrapped green bag emblazoned with their name as a souvenir and an apology for the noise caused by ongoing renovations; then off to inspect the RENFE broad gauge station, with its magnificent stained class tribute to Basque society (miners, fishermen, winnowers, pelote players etc), and then back across the bridge admiring the single-track Bilbotram (this place appears to have been colonised by Tolkien maniacs) to the Cafe Bilbao under the arches of the big square, which offers a truly worthwhile breakfast with good tortilla.

figure.head: Um, one of them, one of them, and a cafe con leche please

div.p: Back to the hotel for a 1030 departure, pick up bags, wave goodbye, avoid falling down the staircase yet again, and it’s off to Atxari in the rain (again). What else can one say? Not much really. Two tickets to Donostia and it’s another 150 minutes stop-everywhere 4-coach Euskotren electric multiple unit, which people get on and off at frequent intervals as we wind up and down valleys, along and not along the coast, turning from double to single track, and heading for what my Italian guidebook claims — under the heading of San Sebastian — is a ridiculously overpriced resort for nouveaux riches.

figure.head: Yes, that is Don Quixote and Sancho Panza trying to upstage us in this carefully organised photo on the front in San Sebastian

div.p: We do however find some two-o-clock lunch for only twice the average cost so far, and then wander along to the seafront where Lou is able to practice his French on a gentleman whom he convinces to take our photo in front of the local statue of Don Quixote and Sancho Panza. And we find a bun for tea before boarding the 1545 for Hendaye and saying farewell to Spanish (if not Basque) soil.

We are booked into different Hendaye hotels under the same management, with shared pricing and breakfasting arrangements. Visit to the station to renew my French Carte Senior+, then a stroll along the old port, commanding magnificent views of Spain, plus several antique cannons to fire at it. Back into town through the seaside villas in search of a place for dinner. Three storeys of wooden gallery in the church from which the faithful are just emerging (Lou witnessed the transubstantiation, i.e. saw the priest quaffing the wine; Guy was too scared to watch). Back in the square an establishment with the unpromising name of Pizzeria 64 has a Gault&Millau review in its window, and comes up with a bottle of Basque cider (aired using the plastic top method), as well as an entirely acceptable gigot d’agneau and poulet basquaise, though we’re not sure about the guffawing landlord. And so back to the Hotel de la Gare, where the Basque digestif could better have been an omitted experience. And we fix breakfast for 8 in order to further demonstrate our astounding efficiency, even where no narrow gauge is any longer involved.

div.head: In which we glide back into the 21st century
div.p: Lou says: An early start in Hendaye, where since it is Thursday the artisanal boulangerie is shut. But breakfast in the cafe de la gare is sympathique and the petit déjeuner is offert (Madame said pityingly when we arrived, “parce que vous êtes tous seuls, les deux”) so not to be spurned. We bid farewell to the Basque country, which is just waking up, and board the 0910 idTGV to Paris. Our carriage is at the very very end of the platform, in an almost deserted espace idZen (i.e. quiet zone), so we choose the comfiest best placed seats available and take in the view all the way to Bordeaux (stopping at St Jean de Luz, Bayonne, and Dax) at which point the train fills up and we have to return to our allocated seats, curses curses. The view is however fairly unspectacular: the route takes us briefly along the coast through some bosky bays, but then turns inland and proceeds in an absolutely straight line across what I surmise was once a flood plain to Bordeaux. Guy (who has bought a map) observes that the straight line deviates whenever necessary in order to cross a river at exactly 90 degrees.

After Bordeaux, one of my fellow passengers complains that I am typing too loudly, but another graciously restores to me the passport I had accidentally dropped when changing my seat. So honour is satisfied. I contemplate the green fields, modest woods, villages, and occasional chateaux of Aquitaine disappearing rapidly from view outside the window, and ponder the wonders of modern science which enable me to do so at the same time as engaging in badinage with my daughters via Whatsapp. Well, perhaps “ponder” is too strong a word. And in a final feat of tail-consuming bravado, I try to complete my final blog entry for this trip, without hitting the keys too hard or too fast. Speeding across France by TGV is becoming so routine I am not sure what to write about it: it’s faster and mostly more comfortable than the FEVE, obviously, but you miss the sense of proximity to the landscape that slower speed and frequent wiggling confer. So I scarcely notice when our train finally gets to the high speed line north of Bordeaux and hits its top speed of around 300 km/hr. At 15h33 precisely we are at Paris Montparnasse (at the very front of the train now, which is an unexpected bonus), and being swallowed up by the general rush to the metro: line 6 for Guy, who is going to rest for the night before proceeding back to Italy; line 4 for me, as I am going on to Paris Nord, for the Eurostar to London. So farewells are said, and we disappear into the crowd.

Fortunately there are (a few) bits of FEVE left which neither Guy nor I has yet explored; plus a few places that seemed worth a second visit (Ferrol and Leon, notably) so we’ll just have to come back some time.
